planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm (65°F+); in zone 9 plant March–June. Okra thrives in zone 9's hot, humid summers and is one of the most productive and reliable summer vegetables for the Gulf South — plants can produce continuously for months in summer heat. Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination; okra seeds have a hard seed coat that benefits from scarification. Harvest pods every 2–3 days when 2–4 inches long — larger pods become fibrous and tough and should be removed to keep the plant producing. Mature plants (3–6 feet) may need support in wind-prone locations.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — okra thrives in the same hot, humid conditions that challenge most other vegetables; Clemson Spineless is the most widely grown okra variety in the United States, a 1980 All-America Selections winner and the standard against which all other okras are measured — smooth, bright green, spineless pods in the classic okra shape eliminate the spine irritation that makes harvesting traditional okra varieties uncomfortable. The spineless pods (technically very few small, soft spines compared to original okra types) can be harvested without gloves, a significant practical improvement over older varieties. Clemson Spineless produces straight, uniform 5-ribbed pods with the mild, slightly mucilaginous flavor that defines okra — the mucilage (a natural thickener) is the basis for gumbo's characteristic body-giving quality that makes real gumbo unmistakably different from any other soup. In zone 9 okra is among the most productive and reliable summer vegetables available — it is native to Africa and genuinely thrives in the same conditions (intense heat, humidity, heat soil) that defeat cool-season crops and stress many warm-season vegetables.
sources: All-America Selections — Clemson Spineless Okra 1939 and 1980 Awards
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Okra Production for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Okra Varieties for Texas

planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm (65°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September for fall production. Lima beans require warm soil and warm nights to develop properly — cool temperatures cause poor pod set and seed development. Fordhook 242 is a bush type that requires no support; harvest pods when seeds are plump and green but before the pods yellow. Shelling fresh lima beans immediately after harvest produces the sweetest, most tender flavor — the sugars begin converting to starch within hours of harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — lima beans fix atmospheric nitrogen and improve soil quality for subsequent crops; Fordhook 242 is the standard large-seeded bush lima bean — an All-America Selections award-winning variety from the Burpee Fordhook line that has defined the Fordhook (large, flat, meaty-seeded) type of lima bean in American horticulture for nearly a century. The large, flat, creamy-green seeds are the classic 'butter bean' of Southern cooking — much larger and meatier than baby lima beans, with a rich, starchy, almost buttery flavor and firm-yet-creamy texture that makes them one of the most satisfying of all shelling beans. Lima beans are often unfairly associated with canned or frozen product quality that gives no sense of their fresh garden character — freshly shelled and immediately cooked Fordhook lima beans have a completely different, far superior flavor to processed versions, with genuine sweetness and a rich, filling character. The large seeds also dry well for winter use, expanding culinary possibilities beyond the fresh-shelled season.
sources: All-America Selections — Fordhook Lima Bean Award
W. Atlee Burpee Company — Fordhook 242 Lima Bean
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Lima Beans for Gulf South Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Blue Zinger sedge tolerates a wide range of light and moisture conditions — from dry shade to moist, sunny locations — making it one of the most versatile ground cover plants available. In zone 9 it performs as a reliable evergreen perennial, maintaining its distinctive blue-gray foliage through the year. The blue coloration is most vivid in spring and fall; summer heat may slightly mute the blue intensity. Division every 3–4 years in spring maintains vigor. An excellent choice for replacing lawn grass in dry-shade areas under trees.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained to moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Blue Zinger sedge tolerates a remarkably wide range of conditions from dry shade to moist full sun; Blue Zinger is a cultivar of Carex flacca (glaucous sedge or blue sedge), a European sedge species selected for its intensely blue-gray foliage — one of the few genuinely blue-toned ground cover plants for shaded and partially shaded garden areas. The thin, arching, blue-gray leaves form a low, dense, slowly spreading mat that provides an elegant, fine-textured alternative to conventional grass lawn in shaded areas where turf grass fails. Unlike many ornamental grasses and sedges, Blue Zinger tolerates shade well while also performing adequately in full sun — a genuine dual-exposure tolerance that makes it broadly useful across garden microclimates. The distinctive blue-gray foliage color provides a neutral, cooling visual counterpoint to green and gold foliage plants in shade garden combinations, and functions as a living mulch that suppresses weeds, retains moisture, and prevents soil erosion on shaded slopes. As an evergreen perennial, Blue Zinger provides year-round ground coverage in zone 9 with minimal maintenance once established.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Carex flacca Blue Zinger Plant Finder
North Carolina State University Extension — Ornamental Sedges for Shade
University of Georgia Extension — Shade Ground Covers for the South

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Hopi Red Dye amaranth grows very rapidly in warm weather — plants can reach 6 feet or more in zone 9's summer conditions. The tiny seeds can be broadcast over prepared soil and raked in lightly; thin to 18 inches when seedlings are 4–6 inches tall. Harvest seed heads when the seeds begin to turn from dark purple to brownish-red and shatter easily when rubbed between the fingers. Shake or rub seed heads over a container to collect seeds; winnow to remove chaff.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean, neutral soil — amaranth grows in poor soils and does not need fertilization to thrive; Hopi Red Dye is an heirloom grain amaranth variety cultivated by the Hopi people of the American Southwest for centuries — the deep burgundy-red to maroon pigment extracted from the dried seeds and flowers was used as a traditional food dye for piki (paper-thin corn bread) and other ceremonial foods, creating a vivid magenta-red color central to Hopi cultural celebrations. The plant is simultaneously ornamental and productive: growing 5–7 feet tall with dramatic, deep burgundy-red seed heads and richly colored foliage that makes it one of the most striking large-scale garden plants for summer use. The small seeds (grain) are a nutritionally exceptional whole grain — very high in protein (13–17%), lysine (an amino acid deficient in most grains), and various minerals — usable as a cooked grain substitute for rice or quinoa, ground into flour, or popped like popcorn. Young leaves can be eaten as a cooked green similar to spinach. The drought-tolerance and heat-adaptability of amaranth make Hopi Red Dye one of the best grain crops for zone 9's challenging summer conditions.
sources: Native Seeds SEARCH — Hopi Red Dye Amaranth Heritage Preservation
Seed Savers Exchange — Hopi Red Dye Amaranth
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Amaranth as Alternative Crop

planting.note: Plant transplants at or shortly after last frost; in zone 9 plant February–April and again September–November. Snow Princess is a vegetatively propagated variety (sold as transplants only, not seed) selected for superior heat tolerance vs. standard seed-grown alyssum — it continues blooming in summer heat that causes other alyssum to stop flowering. In zone 9 use as a spring and fall annual; it may slow in the hottest summer weeks but typically rebounds when temperatures moderate. The spreading, mounding habit (6–10 inches tall, 18–24 inches wide) makes it excellent for container spillers and front-border edging.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — sweet alyssum thrives in the cooler conditions of partial shade in warm climates and benefits from afternoon protection; Snow Princess is a vegetatively propagated sweet alyssum cultivar developed for exceptional heat tolerance and extended bloom compared to standard seed-grown alyssum varieties. The cascading mounds of tiny, pure white, honey-scented flowers create one of the most intensely fragrant annual flower effects available — a cloud of sweet, delicate honey fragrance that can fill an entire garden space from a relatively small planting. Sweet alyssum's fragrance is particularly concentrated in the cooler parts of the day (morning and evening), intensifying when dew is present or during spring and fall in zone 9. Snow Princess blooms continuously from planting and is self-cleaning — tiny spent flowers drop without deadheading to maintain the pristine white appearance. The spreading habit makes Snow Princess outstanding for container edges, hanging basket trailing, and as a fragrant ground cover between stepping stones where foot traffic releases the characteristic honey scent.
sources: Proven Winners — Snow Princess Sweet Alyssum Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers for Florida Landscapes
North Carolina State University Extension — Fragrant Annual Flowers
